title: How yoga can be retraumatizing without trauma-informed instruction
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I remember my first yoga class. I was twenty-two, freshly out of a relationship that had left me hollowed out. The teacher told us to close our eyes and breath...
metaDescription: I remember my first yoga class. I was twenty-two, freshly out of a relationship that had left me hollowed out. The teacher told us to close our eyes and b...
htmlContent:
I remember my first yoga class. I was twenty-two, freshly out of a relationship that had left me hollowed out. The teacher told us to close our eyes and breathe. I closed my eyes. And I was gone. Not in a transcendent way. In a dissociative way. I left my body completely, floated up near the ceiling, watched the woman below me on the mat from a safe distance. Nobody noticed. Nobody asked. The teacher just kept saying "find your edge" and "breathe into the sensation." I thought I was doing it right. I thought that floating feeling was peace.

It took me years to understand what actually happened in that room. I wasn't relaxing. I was leaving. My nervous system was doing what it learned to do in childhood: disappear when things got too much. And yoga, as it's often taught in the West, can accidentally train you to do exactly that. Stay with me here, because this is where it gets complicated.

The body keeps the score, even when you're in downward dog

Here's the thing. Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His research at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that traumatic experiences don't just live in your memories. They live in your physical sensations, your muscle tension, your breathing patterns, your gut. Your body remembers what your mind has tried to forget.

So when you walk into a yoga studio and the teacher tells you to hold a pose for five breaths, and that pose happens to activate a memory your body has stored, your nervous system can flip. It can go into fight, flight, or freeze. And because you're in a room full of people on mats, and the expectation is to stay calm and breathe, you might freeze. You might dissociate. You might smile and keep going while your heart rate spikes and your vision blurs.

I've done that. I've held warrior two while my mind went completely blank and my hands started tingling. I thought I was "working through it." I thought the discomfort was growth. Sometimes it is. Sometimes it's just re-enactment. Your body re-living an old wound because the conditions are similar enough to trigger it, and nobody is there to catch you.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate's work shows how suppressed stress and unprocessed trauma literally make you sick. Yoga can be part of healing that. But it can also be another way to suppress, if the instruction doesn't account for what's actually happening in your nervous system.

The problem with "find your edge" and other well-meaning phrases

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about the window of tolerance. It's the zone where your nervous system can handle sensation without flipping into hyperarousal (panic, rage, flooding) or hypoarousal (numbness, collapse, dissociation). Trauma narrows this window. Things that wouldn't bother someone else can knock you right out of it.

Now think about common yoga instructions. "Push through the discomfort." "Breathe into the tightness." "Find your edge and stay there." These phrases assume you have a healthy relationship with discomfort. They assume you can distinguish between a muscle stretch and a trauma trigger. They assume your body's signals are reliable.

They're not. Not when you're carrying unprocessed trauma. Not when your nervous system is wired for survival.

I once had a teacher walk over and press on my hips during a seated forward fold. She said "just let go, sweetie." I didn't let go. I went numb. My whole lower body disappeared. I couldn't feel my legs for the rest of the class. I walked out of that studio and sat in my car for twenty minutes, staring at nothing. I didn't know what happened. I just knew I felt wrong. Empty. Gone.

That's retraumatization. It doesn't always look like crying or panic. Sometimes it looks like leaving your body so completely that you can't feel anything at all.

Wild, right? The thing that's supposed to help you feel more embodied can actually teach you to disassociate more effectively. You get better at leaving. You get better at tolerating discomfort by checking out. You become a master of the freeze response, all while thinking you're becoming more mindful.

What trauma-informed yoga actually looks like

Trauma-informed instruction isn't just about being nice. It's not about using soft lighting and gentle music. It's about understanding how the nervous system works and teaching from that understanding. It's about giving you choices instead of commands. It's about watching for signs of dissociation and knowing how to bring someone back.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma and the body, emphasizes that healing requires pendulation. You move toward the difficult sensation, then back to safety. You don't just stay at the edge. You learn to recognize when you've left your window of tolerance and how to return.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down Polyvagal Theory in ways you can actually use. One of the key ideas is that you can't force your way out of a survival state. You have to invite safety. Yoga that isn't trauma-informed often does the opposite. It demands. It pushes. It assumes you can handle it because you're an adult on a mat.

Trauma-informed yoga might look like this: The teacher says "you can close your eyes or keep them open, whatever feels right." They say "notice if you feel pressure to push further than your body wants to go today." They say "if you feel spacey or disconnected, try opening your eyes and looking at one spot in the room." They check in. They adjust. They don't assume.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. It gets into the specifics of how the body processes trauma and what actually helps. The principles apply directly to movement practices like yoga.

How to know if your practice is helping or hurting

This is the hard part. Because trauma lives in the body, and the body's signals are often scrambled. You might feel calm when you're actually dissociating. You might feel strong when you're actually pushing past your limits in a way that reinforces old patterns. You might think you're healing when you're just re-enacting.

Here are some signs that your yoga practice might be retraumatizing you. You feel worse after class, but you can't explain why. You feel numb or disconnected during or after practice. You find yourself crying for reasons you don't understand. You push through pain because you think you should. You compare your practice to others and feel shame. You leave class feeling more disconnected from your body, not less. You feel pressure to perform relaxation, to look peaceful, to be "good" at yoga.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that list. Did something tighten? Did you feel a wave of recognition? That's information.

I'm not saying yoga is bad. I'm saying yoga without understanding trauma is like surgery without understanding anatomy. You might get lucky. You might also cut something vital. The practice itself isn't the problem. The problem is the assumption that everyone in the room has a nervous system that responds the same way. The problem is the assumption that discomfort always means growth. The problem is the lack of training in how to recognize and respond to trauma responses.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). If you're in a class where the music or the teacher's voice feels overwhelming, having control over your auditory environment can make a difference. Small adjustments matter. They signal safety to your nervous system.

What to do if you've been retraumatized by yoga

First, stop blaming yourself. You didn't do yoga wrong. You didn't fail at healing. Your body did exactly what it was designed to do. It protected you the only way it knew how. The problem was the environment, not you.

Second, give yourself permission to stop. You don't have to go back to that studio. You don't have to do yoga at all. There are other ways to connect with your body. Walking. Swimming. Dancing in your kitchen. Lying on the floor and breathing. Anything that lets you feel safe in your own skin.

Third, if you want to try yoga again, look for teachers who explicitly name trauma-informed approaches. Ask questions. "What do you do if someone dissociates during class?" "Do you offer modifications without being asked?" "Do you touch students without consent?" The answers will tell you everything.

Fourth, work with your nervous system directly. Polyvagal theory, somatic experiencing, and internal family systems are all approaches that understand trauma from the body up. You don't have to stretch or pose to heal. You just have to learn how to listen to what your body is actually saying, not what you think it should say.

Sit with that. The idea that healing might not look like what you thought. The idea that slowing down and doing less might be more effective than pushing through. The idea that your body's resistance is not your enemy. It's your guide. It's been trying to protect you all along.

I still do yoga sometimes. But I choose carefully. I keep my eyes open when I need to. I stop when something feels wrong, even if I can't explain why. I let myself be the person who leaves early or sits out a pose. I've stopped trying to be good at yoga. I'm just trying to be present. And sometimes, that means not doing yoga at all.
